content.full_text:
FPPC

MAR 2 3 1992

March 12, 1992
PO Box 4134
San Luis Obispo
Ca. 93403
805-541-2615

Dear FPPC,

San Luis Obispo County has at least two major ballot issues coming up this year, plus many local supervisorial and city council races. I am very active in local political issues and know of a number of people who would like to make some contributions to either supporting or defeating these issues or candidates.

Some people, however, are concerned about having their names appear in public records.

They have asked me to check with the FPPC about a possible legal loophole to allow them to contribute financially above local and state campaign or election law limits and still remain anonymous.

One person showed me a flyer (enclosed) put out by a group of San Luis Obispo County residents last year who supported a measure that was on the June 4, 1991 ballot. Those behind it did not print on the flyer who put it out, but a spokesman for the group claimed they did not have to do so or follow other local and state codes and laws for a very unique legal reason.

The reason given on a local news broadcast was because this flyer and other extensive campaign activities openly used by these people to successfully pass the measure never actually concluded or stated "Vote for Measure A", but simply "Vote on Measure A".

Many people are interested in using this "loophole" and other campaign techniques developed by this group, if the FPPC and other appropriate government agencies rule it is legal. If they do, it would allow them to include people into the political process who may be otherwise afraid to do so.

For example, using the enclosed "Measure A Fact Sheet" as a model, is it possible for a group that supports, say, a particular city council candidate to run positive ads, put out favorable flyers and otherwise campaign for this candidate to do so independent of all campaign laws, as long as they never actually actually conclude "Vote For Council Candidate Smith"? After stating all the favorable facts about their candidate, can they say, "Vote on the City Council race" and avoid these restrictive laws?

Another group I will be working with wants to defeat a county sales tax. Can they raise several thousand dollars for their efforts, spend it and not need to report it if they never actually state anywhere "Vote against the tax measure"?

Also, there is one group I know of that can get some government funding for "educational" purposes. As long as they never actually come out officially for or against the measure involved, can they use tax revenue to campaign for or against the measure, as long as it is educational, as the proponents of Measure A claimed?

It is important to hear from someone at the FPPC who can answer these questions as soon as possible, as these campaigns will be under way very soon.

[This image appears to be a very faded or low-contrast document where the text is largely illegible. While there seems to be body text present throughout the page, the contrast is too poor for me to accurately transcribe the specific words and content with confidence. The document appears to have multiple paragraphs and possibly some formatting, but I cannot reliably read the actual text content.]

Those who campaigned for the Measure A last year may have found an important
exemption to just about all state and local campaign laws and election codes. At least that
is what they claim, since they spent over $50,000 to do so.

I have some serious doubts about this "loophole", however, and have told people I
would check into it before someone misinterprets the law.

Thank you for your prompt reply.

Sincerely,

Ron Bearce
Ron Bearce

MEASURE "A" FACT SHEET

San Luis Coastal Unified School District has placed Measure "A" on the
June 4, 1991, local election ballot. Measure "A" is a $50 million bond
issue which will allow the district additional uses for a previously
approved tax rate increase.

DISTRICT ENROLLMENT IS INCREASING. Student enrollment is rising and projections indicate
that San Luis Coastal Unified School District must find room for an additional 1,244
students during the next five years. The district needs the additional funds the bond will
provide in order to keep pace with expanding enrollment.

THE QUALITY OF EDUCATION IS IMPORTANT TO EVERYONE IN SAN LUIS OBISPO
COUNTY. Measure "A" will allow the district to make improvements at every school within
San Luis Coastal Unified School District. These improvements will provide a better
educational environment for both students and teachers at each of our school sites.

NONE OF THE BOND MONIES WILL BE USED FOR ADMINISTRATOR SALARIES. All of the
money raised from Measure "A" will go directly to local school facilities. The money cannot
be used for salaries or any other operating expense.

THE BOND MONIES WILL BE USED TO make renovations at schools throughout the district,
modernize math and science wings at district high schools, and bring playground equipment
and fields in the district up to state safety standards.

INSTRUCTIONAL PROGRAMS WILL SUFFER. Due to insufficient funds, the district has cut
student programs and services during the last two years. If the bond does not pass, money
that could be used for instructional programs will have to be used for new construction.

THE LOTTERY WILL NOT PAY FOR BUILDING REPAIRS. State law forbids the use of lottery
funds for construction projects.
